examples.title: Abstract Class Example - Shape Hierarchy
examples.description: Demonstrates abstract class with pure virtual functions and derived class implementations
examples.explanation: This program demonstrates pure virtual functions creating an abstract Shape class. Circle and Rectangle are derived classes that implement the pure virtual functions. The base class pointer array demonstrates runtime polymorphism where the correct area() and perimeter() functions are called based on the actual object type.
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) where n is number of derived classes stored

examples.title: Object Slicing Demonstration
examples.description: Shows what happens when object slicing occurs and how to prevent it
examples.explanation: This code demonstrates object slicing. When a Derived object is passed by value to a function expecting a Base parameter, only the Base portion is copied - the Derived portion is 'sliced off'. Using pointers or references prevents slicing and maintains polymorphism.
examples.spaceComplexity: O(1) for reference/pointer, O(1) for pass-by-value copy

examples.title: Factory Pattern using Pure Virtual Functions
examples.description: Demonstrates Factory Design Pattern implementation with abstract classes
examples.explanation: The Factory Pattern demonstrates practical use of pure virtual functions. Product is an abstract interface defining the contract. Laptop and Phone are concrete implementations. The factory creates objects without the client knowing the exact class, promoting loose coupling and extensibility.
examples.timeComplexity: O(1) for object creation and method calls
examples.spaceComplexity: O(1) for factory, O(n) for product storage

examples.title: Pure Virtual Function with Default Implementation
examples.description: Shows how pure virtual functions can have implementations that derived classes can optionally use
examples.explanation: This demonstrates that pure virtual functions can have implementations. Derived1 overrides completely, while Derived2 explicitly calls the base class implementation using the scope resolution operator (::). This allows providing default behavior while still requiring derived classes to provide their own implementation.
